title: Board of Peace Eight-Month Disarmament Plan Text Published; Hamas Counters With Three-Year Alternative
detail: On April 20, 2026, details of the Board of Peace's full disarmament plan for Gaza became more widely reported by the Times of Israel and other outlets. Under the plan, Hamas would be required to hand over heavy weaponry — rockets, rocket launchers, anti-tank missiles — plus maps of the Gaza tunnel network within 90 days (roughly three months), followed by a broader demilitarization process over eight months total. A buy-back program would offer employment and financial incentives to Qassam Brigades fighters who surrendered personal weapons. In direct contrast, Hamas had advanced a counter-proposal calling for a three-year disarmament timeline beginning with heavy weapons in year one while retaining light arms for self-defense during a transitional period. The IDF stated it expected Hamas to 'soon enter the disarmament process' and anticipated a handover of heavy weapons and tunnel maps in early May 2026 — though Hamas had given no public indication of accepting this timeline. The US maintained that Phase Two negotiations — covering a permanent ceasefire and Gaza's post-war political future — would not begin until the disarmament process was formally underway. The eight-month versus three-year gap remained the central impasse in Phase Two talks.

media.caption: Text of Board of Peace Gaza plan calls for Hamas to disarm over eight months — Times of Israel

title: Hamas Confirms Qassam Brigades Arsenal Excluded From Any Handover; IDF Rejects Proposal
detail: On April 20, 2026, the Jerusalem Post published detailed reporting confirming that Hamas's proposed limited handover of police and internal security weapons explicitly preserved the Izz ad-Din al-Qassam Brigades' full military arsenal, including rockets, drones, anti-tank missiles, and tunnel infrastructure. Hamas officials stated that governance transfers to the Board of Peace's technocratic committee were being coordinated for civilian services — but that the Qassam Brigades' weapons would not be included in any near-term arrangement. The IDF rejected any proposal to merely 'store' rather than surrender weapons, insisting that full military disarmament was non-negotiable and that partial handovers would not unlock reconstruction funding or IDF withdrawal. The FDD documented Hamas's continued refusal to disarm its military wing as placing the Gaza ceasefire 'in jeopardy.' Despite the impasse, Cairo backchannel contacts between Hamas, US, Qatari, Egyptian, and Turkish mediators were reported to be continuing. The structural gap — Hamas's demand for a political pathway to statehood before disarmament versus the Board of Peace's requirement of disarmament before political progress — remained unresolved.

media.caption: Hamas confirms Qassam Brigades excluded from any weapons handover; IDF rejects proposal — Jerusalem Post
